[[ja:岩村田祇園祭]]

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

[[ja:岩村田祇園祭]]

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.

The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.

{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

revision.text:
[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]

* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]

== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}

* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24年}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58年}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24th year }}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}

In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5th year }} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58th year }}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24th year }}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5th year }} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58th year }}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24th year }}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.

[[ja:お船祭り (須々岐水神社)]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5th year }} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58th year }}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24th year }}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Natsu Matsuri|12=draft}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saku City|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城|12=draft}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)|12=draft}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺|12=draft}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5th year|12=draft}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏|12=draft}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|12=draft}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒|12=draft}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58th year|12=draft}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24th year|12=draft}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]

It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by Saku City.
== History ==
According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城|12=draft}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)|12=draft}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|white heron|ja|白鷺|12=draft}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: - **Shishi Rock** (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. - **Ofune-ga-Fuchi** (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. - **Tsunegi** (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. - **Tateishi** (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. - **Ofune Stone** (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5th year|12=draft}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏|12=draft}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsunashima Shrine|ja|津島神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|12=draft}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒|12=draft}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices ==
On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58th year|12=draft}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24th year|12=draft}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水|12=draft}}

The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups. The event is locally referred to as "Ogyonsaa" (おぎょんさあ) or "Ofune-san" (おふねさん), with the pronunciation de-emphasizing the "fu" sound.
== Timeline ==
* **Ōei 5 (1398)**: Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * **Eiroku era (1558–1570)**: Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * **Kan'ei era (1624–1644)**: Festival revived after 70 years. * **Kyōhō 2 (1716)**: Fights during the festival become problematic. * **Tenpō 5 (1834)**: Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * **Meiji era (1868)**: Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * **Shōwa 16 (1941)**: Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling ==
Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations ==
* In {{ill|Shōwa 58 (1983)|ja|昭和58th year|12=draft|12=draft}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|Heisei 24 (2012)|ja|平成24th year|12=draft|12=draft}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
